coredata.dc:title: Controlling speciation of nitrogen in nitrogen-doped carbon dots by ferric ion catalysis for enhancing fluorescence
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Ruoxin
Li, Lili
Jin, Wei Jun
coredata.dc:description: The speciation of doped-nitrogen (pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, graphitic N and amino N) was controlled by ferric ion catalysis for improving fluorescence of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs). Comparing to the synthesis of NCDs without ferric ion catalysis, the present strategy induces two following effects: (1) an increase of pyrrolic N and a decrease of pyridinic N; (2) a more well-organized arrangement of graphitic core and more N moieties exposed on the surface of NCDs. Consequently, absolute fluorescence quantum yield is increased from 6.2% to 27.0% and fluorescence can be further enhanced in acidity-media. Such phenomena provide solid evidence that pyrrolic N enhances the fluorescence of NCDs while pyridinic N inhibits it. Besides, NCDs achieve selective response to ferric ions. The present work provides a deeper insight into the understanding of luminescence mechanism and causes broader interest in manipulating CDs chemical structure.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Response to pH and metal ions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 90922023 ) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China. We would also like to express our sincere gratitude and appreciation to Yu Ye, for her assistance in spectral measurements, who graduated her Bachelor's degree from Beijing Normal University in 2014. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of Fe30-NCDs. Inset: HRTEM images and size distribution. The bar = 10 nm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XRD patterns of p-NCDs (No.0, black), Fe10-NCDs (No.1, brown) and Fe30-NCDs (No.2, red), the latter two as representatives for Fe-NCDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) XPS spectra of Fe-NCDs and p-NCDs. (b) Atomic proportion of nitrogen (red-filled) and oxygen (grey-shaded) in Fe-NCDs and p-NCDs. (c) Normalized high-resolution XPS spectra of N1s: p-NCDs (No.0, black-dashed), Fe10-NCDs (No.1, blue), Fe20-NCDs (No.2, green), Fe30-NCDs (No.3, red), and Fe40-NCDs (No.4, orange). (d) Ideal schematic local structures of Fe-NCD and p-NCDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Normalized FT-IR spectra of synthesized NCDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of p-NCDs (No.0, black), Fe10-NCDs (No.1, blue), Fe20-NCDs (No.2, green), Fe30-NCDs (No.3, red) and Fe40-NCDs (No.4, orange), respectively (the same blow). (b) PL emission spectra of NCDs. Inset: photographs of NCDs under ambient light (up) and UV-365 nm lamp (down). (c) Normalized PL excitation spectra of NCDs. (d) Fluorescence decays of NCDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The relationship between (I−I 0)/I 0 and pH value at the corresponding maximum emission of the p-NCDs (triangle, black), Fe10-NCDs (square, red), Fe20-NCDs (cross, blue) and Fe30-NCDs (circle, green), respectively. I and I 0 refer to the PL intensity of NCDs at pH less than 7.0 and at 7.0. (b) Normalized XPS spectra of N1s of Fe10-NCDs at pH 7.0 (dashed, black) and pH 3.0 (red). (c) Fluorescence decay of the Fe10-NCDs at pH 7.0 (hollow, blue) and pH 3.0 (solid, red). All the lifetimes of samples are fitted as bi-exponential decay kinetics. (d) Schematic proposal mechanism of the suppressing PET process from surface pyridinic N by protons. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
PL intensity of synthesized NCDs by catalysis of various metal ions. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The variation of PL intensity of Fe10-NCDs at pH 1.2 responded to different metal ions with concentration of 300 μmol L−1. (b) PL emission spectra of Fe10-NCDs at pH 1.2 upon addition of various amounts of Fe3+ (from top to bottom: 0, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 100, 150, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600, 700, 800, 900 and 1000 μmol L−1). (c) The relationship between the variation I/I 0 of PL intensity of Fe10-NCDs and the concentration of Fe3+. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Percentages of N atomic speciation in Fe-NCDs and p-NCDs.
Main photophysical properties of Fe-NCDs and p-NCDs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Notes: λ Em refers to PL emission peak position; Φ: absolute fluorescence quantum yield; τ 1 and τ 2: fluorescent lifetimes from component 1 and 2, τ A: average fluorescence lifetime; k r: rate constant of radiative transition, k nr: rate constant of non-radiative transition.

head.ce:title: Controlling speciation of nitrogen in nitrogen-doped carbon dots by ferric ion catalysis for enhancing fluorescence

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Beijing Normal University, Beijing 100875, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The speciation of doped-nitrogen (pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, graphitic N and amino N) was controlled by ferric ion catalysis for improving fluorescence of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs). Comparing to the synthesis of NCDs without ferric ion catalysis, the present strategy induces two following effects: (1) an increase of pyrrolic N and a decrease of pyridinic N; (2) a more well-organized arrangement of graphitic core and more N moieties exposed on the surface of NCDs. Consequently, absolute fluorescence quantum yield is increased from 6.2% to 27.0% and fluorescence can be further enhanced in acidity-media. Such phenomena provide solid evidence that pyrrolic N enhances the fluorescence of NCDs while pyridinic N inhibits it. Besides, NCDs achieve selective response to ferric ions. The present work provides a deeper insight into the understanding of luminescence mechanism and causes broader interest in manipulating CDs chemical structure.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nano-materials such as carbon nanotubes, graphene sheets, graphene oxide have inspired extensive studies about their unique properties [1–7]. Since its discovery in 2004 [8], carbon dots (CDs), as a common carbon-based fluorescent nano-material, has received growing attention due to its excellent optical and non-toxic features [9] as well as its great potential in a wide range of applications [10–12]. It is considered that CDs consist of a graphene-based core and abundant surface functional groups, and more and more studies have shown that the latter is closely related to luminescence of CDs [13–15]. Recently, “bottom-up” method to prepare CDs by using small organic molecule or ‘‘green’’ natural raw materials have become mainstream due to low cost and convenience. However, it exhibits low fluorescence quantum yields (QY) of CDs and requires subsequent strategies including surface passivation or functionalization to modify that [1,16]. Moreover, many researchers believe that heteroatom doping, especially nitrogen doping, can effectively improve photoluminescence (PL) of CDs [17,18]. Although nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs) can be easily synthesized using various ingredients and simple methods [19–22], not all of them can achieve a high quality of PL [23–25].

Actually, different content, ratios or speciation of doped N could induce different influence on PL properties of NCDs. Exploring the structural essence of nitrogen species is the key to improving quality of NCDs. Du et al. [17] concluded that only aromatic N, which can be incorporated into carbon framework, is real dopants; while the amino N in aliphatic chains only works as a surface passivator. Qian et al. [26,27] compared a series NCDs derived from different precursors and proposed that amino and pyrrolic N atoms can enhance PL by generating more protonation process or aromatic structures, which facilitate radiative recombination. Arcudi et al. [18] reported that reduced amino groups can enhance QY of NCDs, despite most researchers believe that amino groups enhance PL through self-passivation [26,28]. Ding et al. [29] incorporated a small amount of nitrogen atoms into S-doped CDs and deduced that improved PL emission is ascribed to both CN and CN bonds inside carbonous cores. Sarker et al. [30] presented a systematic theoretical study and proved that only graphitic N can effectively tailor the electronic energy levels by electron-doping effect. Guo et al. [31] introduced quaternary ammonium on CDs surface states which led to remarkable red-shift of PL emission. However, the chemical structure and function of doped nitrogen still received limited attention, which can actually improve PL. Therefore, further study is needed to clearly identify the key factors which contribute to PL by N-doping and effectively control them to improve PL properties of NCDs. It is important to design appropriate proposal for N doping and understand the PL mechanism and surface chemistry of NCDs.

There are various methods to control structure and property of CDs, for examples, regulating chemical composition of CDs by using different precursors [26,27,31–33], normalizing size and morphology of CDs by templates [34–36], adjusting growth speed of CDs by inorganic molecule [37–39], etc. Herein, the present work proposes a feasible and effective method to controlling N speciation for improving PL of NCDs by ferric ion catalysis. A series of NCDs were designed using l-histidine as both carbon and nitrogen sources in the present of different amounts of Fe3+ as catalysts under hydrothermal condition. Comparing to pristine NCDs without ferric ion catalysis (p-NCDs), the absolute QY of Fe3+-catalyzed NCDs (Fe-NCDs) can be improved from 6.2% to 27.0% without any surface passivation and then the fluorescence intensity can be further enhanced in acidity-media. With the aid of Fe3+ catalysis, clear evidence is given that more pyrrolic N and less pyridinic N play vital roles in improving PL property of NCDs. And a more well-organized arrangement of graphitic core and more N moieties exposed on the surface of NCDs also facilitate PL enhancement. Moreover, other metal ions as catalysts are also investigated to demonstrate the feasibility and universality of this method. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time that metal ions are proposed only acting as catalysts to control the structure of NCDs. Besides, Fe-NCDs achieve selective response to Fe3+ at μmol L−1 level.

l-histidine, l-lysine, l-cysteine, glycine and ruthenium (III) chloride hydrate were obtained from J&K Scientific Ltd (Beijing, China). Iron (III) and iron (II) perchlorate hydrate, zinc (II) perchlorate, chromium (III) perchlorate hexahydrate, manganese (II) perchlorate, copper (II) perchlorate, nickel (II) perchlorate hexahydrate, cobalt (II) perchlorate, lead (II) perchlorate trihydrate, and d-(+)-galactose were purchased from Alfa Aesar (China) Chemical Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Cadmium (II) chloride and aluminum chloride were from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Mercury (II) nitrate hydrate, silver (I) nitrate, hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide were from Beijing Chemical Reagent Plant (Beijing, China). Spectra/Por® Biotech Dialysis Membranes (MWCO, 1 K) were procured from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai) Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm @25 °C) used in all the experiments was produced using a Milli-Q water system.

Fe-NCDs were prepared from l-histidine in the present of Fe3+ through a simple and convenient one-step hydrothermal method. Briefly, l-histidine (0.2485 g, 1.6 mmol) and a certain amount of Fe(ClO4)3·H2O were dissolved in ultrapure water (8 mL) and then sonicated for 30 min. Four levels of Fe(ClO4)3·H2O were carried out, as 0.0595 g (0.16 mmol), 0.119 g (0.32 mmol), 0.1785 g (0.48 mmol) and 0.2380 g (0.64 mmol), correspondingly the molar ratios of Fe3+ to l-histidine were 10%, 20%, 30% and 40%, respectively. Then the precursor solution was sealed in a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, followed by hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 4 h. The collected products were filtered first to remove carbon impurities and a part of iron salts or oxide. Then, the filtrates were stored for a few days so that colloidal iron impurities could precipitate out. During this period, the solutions were filtered through filtration membrane (0.22 μm) once a day to clear the precipitation away. In the case of 10% Fe3+ (molar ratio to l-histidine), it took seven days to get rid of the precipitation; for 40% Fe3+, it only took two days. That is, the more Fe3+ added into reaction, the less settling time was needed. Until iron impurities removed up, the Fe-NCDs solutions were further purified for 2 days using dialysis membrane (MWCO, 1 K) against ultrapure water which was renewed every 12 h.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were performed on JEOL JEM-2100 microscope at accelerating voltage of 100 kV. The heights of NCDs were characterized by Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) using a Nanoscope IIIa Dimension 3100. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was taken on a PANalytical X′ Pert PRO MPD X-ray diffractometer. Raman spectra were recorded using a Horiba Jvon Yvon LabRAM Aramis laser confocal micro-Raman spectroscopy, with laser excitation source at 532 nm. All samples were tested in a droplet form on silicon substrates. Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) was used by a Mettler Toledo TGA/DSC 1 analyzer under nitrogen atmosphere at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu IRAffinity-1 FT-IR spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was investigated by a Thermo Fisher Scientific ESCALab 250Xi spectrometer with AlKα/MgKα excitation. The peak-fit processing was carried out using XPSPEAK Version 4.1 software. Elemental analysis was detected by a Thermo Flash EA1112 elemental analyzer. UV–Visible absorption spectra were collected on a Purkinje TU-1901 spectrophotometer. PL spectra were obtained on a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrometer. Time-resolved fluorescence measurements were performed on an Edinburgh OB920 single-photon counting fluorometer by using a 368 nm laser lamp. Absolute QYs were detected by a Horiba Jobin Yvon FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer with an integrating sphere. These experiments were carried out in a 10-mm quartz cuvette. The pH was determined by a Hanna HI2211 pH/OPR meter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response to pH and metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The pH of dilute solution of Fe-NCDs was adjusted by adding hydrochloric acid (1 mol L−1) and subsequently the corresponding PL spectra were recorded. Different metal ions with a final concentration of 300 μmol L−1 were mixed with Fe-NCDs solution at pH 1.2 to evaluate PL variation. A detection of Fe3+ in Fe-NCDs solution at pH 1.2 was carried out with the final concentration of Fe3+ in the range of 0–1000 μmol L−1. All spectra and data are obtained with excitation wavelength at 340 nm and emission wavelength at 417 nm.

XRD shows that there is only one prominent broad diffraction peak at around 22.8° in Fe-NCDs (Fig. 2 ), corresponding to an interlayer distance of 0.39 nm close to bulk graphite (0.34 nm). The increase is due to abundant surface functional groups of NCDs [41]. Comparing to p-NCDs, which represents another shoulder peak at 30.7°, the XRD patterns of Fe-NCDs appear to be smoother, which indicates a more uniform and smaller interlayer distance. It means Fe3+ catalysis can affect the graphitic core structure of NCDs probably by reducing heteroatoms inside. In Raman spectra, both Fe-NCDs and p-NCDs exhibit two peaks at around 1330 and 1580 cm−1 under the background of strong fluorescence (Fig. S3), corresponding to the well-defined D band and G band of graphitic structure, respectively [42]. The intensity ratio of D band to G band (I D/G) of Fe10-NCDs (I D/G = 0.57) is lower than that of p-NCDs (I D/G = 0.60) (the inset of Fig. S3), which indicates that partial sp2 domains are restored in some ways in Fe10-NCDs [43]. For Fe30-NCDs, I D/G decreases to 0.51, probably due to higher graphitization degree. Several stages of weight loss in TGA of all the NCDs indicate abundant surface functional groups as shown in Fig. S4. The temperatures of 5% weight loss are more than 200 °C, which prove good thermal stability. All Fe-NCDs begin to lose weight at 130 °C, whereas p-NCDs at 180 °C. It implies certain surface structures of Fe-NCDs are clearly different from p-NCDs. From the above, it can be inferred that Fe3+ catalysis mainly induces the differences among chemical structures of all the NCDs.

The structural information of elements and surface state of the synthesized NCDs were obtained by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. It indicates that all Fe-NCDs contain a lower atomic percentage of N and O than p-NCDs and no characteristic peak of Fe element has been detected at 710 eV or 724 eV (Fig. 3 a and b). Comparing to p-NCDs, the N content of Fe10-NCDs drops from 26.8% to 19.2% and the N/C ratio decreases, and both then trend to rise with increased amounts of Fe3+ (Table S2), which was doubly confirmed by elemental analysis (Table S3). Furthermore, the deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectra of N1s, C1s, and O1s confirm that both Fe-NCDs and p-NCDs contain abundant surface functional groups, but also reveal the significant difference of chemical speciation among the NCDs (Fig. S5). It means Fe3+, only as a catalyst, does affect doping of heteroatoms.

Three major types of nitrogen as dopants which contribute to delocalized conjugation systems of NCDs are mainly discussed here: pyridinic N (398.1 eV), pyrrolic N (400.3 eV) and graphitic N (401.2 eV) [43–45]. As shown in Fig. 3c and Table 1 , with an increased level of Fe3+, the proportion of pyridinic N gradually declines from 34.8% to 23.2% and the pyrrolic N ascends from 35.5% to 47.6%. Comparing with pyridinic N, if pyrrolic N incorporates into graphitic framework, it could cause more damage to the integrity of the core of carbon dots than pyridinic N, due to the special five-membered ring. According to the XRD results, Fe-NCDs possesses a more well-organized sp2 carbon arrangement of graphitic core and a more uniform interlayer distance of NCDs than p-NCDs. Thus, it could be inferred that the increased pyrrolic N are distributed on the edge of graphitic framework rather than in the core. Moreover, the apparent blue-shift of the position of pyridinic N implies the difference of chemical environments of pyridinic N, and the reduction of graphitic N doped in graphitic framework indicates that the proportion of N element inside carbonous cores decreases. It suggests that the catalytic effect of Fe3+ transfers more aromatic N from the core to the surface of NCDs. Besides, Fe3+ catalysis also slightly affects oxygen configurations, from CO to CO bond (Fig. S6 and Table S4). Taken into all the factors, the ideal schematic models of local structures of Fe-NCDs and p-NCDs are speculated and displayed in Fig. 3d, which reveal apparent differences of N doping.

Likewise, FT-IR shows information of functional groups of all NCDs. As shown in Fig. 4 , the broad absorption bands at 3411 and 3125 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of OH, NH and CH [46]. The mixed strong band at 1625 cm−1 are assigned to CO, CN and CC [15]. The bands at 1300 to 1500 cm−1 represent aromatic nitrogen heterocycles [47,48]. The peak centered at 1092 cm−1 is a characteristic of bending vibration of CO bond. The FT-IR patterns reveal that with more Fe3+ catalysis, the absorption of OH and NH is stronger and the ratio of CN (1340 cm−1) [49] to CNH (1395 cm−1) [50] is significantly lower, which corresponds to the changes in the XPS results. These results demonstrate that besides quality of starting materials, catalytic effect of metal ions can also control chemical speciation of heteroatoms in NCDs.

The alteration of surface states can significantly affect photophysical nature of CDs [13]. Because of the catalytic effect of Fe3+, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the synthesized NCDs display distinct differences (Fig. 5 a). Along with a weakened peak at 300 nm (n-π* transition of CO) [51], two obvious shoulder peaks appear at 270 nm (π-π* of CN) [29,44] and 350 nm, which is caused by a nitrogen-rich environment on the surface of NCDs [28].

Moreover, PL quality of NCDs is prominent enhanced due to Fe3+ catalysis (Fig. 5b). Both Fe-NCDs and p-NCDs achieve an excitation-wavelength-dependence emission (Fig. S7a and b), which is a typical PL feature of CDs [52]. In contrast to p-NCDs, the dominated emission and excitation wavelengths of all Fe-NCDs blue-shift approximately 20 nm (Fig. 5b and c). It may be due to a higher amount of doped N exposed on the surface of Fe-NCDs, as mentioned in the XPS results, which causes a strong negative inductive effect [53]. Whether excitation wavelength is 340 nm (optimal excitation wavelength of Fe-NCDs) or 360 nm (optimal excitation wavelength of p-NCDs), the corresponding PL emission intensity of Fe-NCDs is higher than p-NCDs (Fig. S7c). Furthermore, absolute fluorescence QYs (Φ) and fluorescence decays of the samples were detected and the details are shown in Table 2 . Fe30-NCDs has the highest absolute fluorescence QY of 27.0% which is 4.4 times higher of p-NCDs. Such QY is close to other excellent CDs in previous works [15,32,54], and it is also expected to surpass some unique quantum dots [55,56] through further surface modification. The fluorescence decays of all samples are fitted as bi-exponential decay kinetics and the average fluorescence lifetimes (τ A) of NCDs shorten from 2.24 to 1.89 ns because of Fe3+ catalysis (Fig. 5d and Table 2). Besides, the interference of acidity from the source Fe3+ was ruled out and the influence of reaction conditions on photophysical properties of NCDs and function of Fe3+ catalysis was investigated (more details can be found in section 1 in the supplementary information).

Combined with the structure information discussed above, it can be concluded that the remarkable enhancement of surface-state-related PL of Fe-NCDs is caused by tunable chemical speciation of nitrogen: a decreased level of pyridinic N and an increased level of pyrrolic N. It is known that doping N prefers to be distributed on the surface of Fe-NCDs. Both pyridinic N and pyrrolic N can affect the large conjugated structure on the edge of graphitic framework and generate more surface defect states which dominate surface-state-related PL of NCDs [27]. For conjugated structural characteristics of the edge of graphitic framework, the impact of pyrrolic N is greater than that of pyridinic N, due to the fact that pyrrolic N as a sp3 hybrid results in poorer planarity of graphitic framework. For the impacts on PL property of NCDs, pyridinic N contains a lone electron pair outside the pyridinic ring plane which will cause more remarkable non-radiative recombination to quench PL by photo-induced electron transfer process (PET); In contrast, pyrrolic N donates two p-electrons to pyrrolic ring and does not possess lone pair electrons. To sum up, although both pyridinic N and pyrrolic N generate surface defect states of NCDs, the former has adverse influence on surface-state-related PL but the latter benefits that. Thus, the rate constant of non-radiative transition (k nr) of Fe-NCDs becomes smaller as pyridinic N decreases. However, with an increased level of Fe3+ catalysis, more pyrrolic N on the surface of NCDs can facilitate the radiative recombination of localized electron–hole pairs [17,41], which results in shorter PL lifetimes and larger rate constants of radiative transition (k r) (Table 2). In contrast, pyridinic N causes more non-radiative recombination due to its lone electron pair outside the ring plane. Thus, the rate constant of non-radiative transition (k nr) of Fe-NCDs becomes smaller as pyridinic N decreases. By comparison of the variation of k r and k nr, it can be found that pyrrolic N makes greater contribution to PL. In addition, comparing with p-NCDs, it is proved that the speciation of nitrogen in NCDs is definitely more effective than the total quantity of nitrogen in improving PL. Although the total N contents and the N/C ratios of Fe-NCDs are lower than p-NCDs, all Fe-NCDs contain higher QYs and apparent blue-shifts of emission spectra. It is probably because more aromatic N distribute on the surface of Fe-NCDs and the flat structure inside the core of Fe-NCDs is more perfect which contributes to PL emission [57].

To further verify that controllable N speciation is mainly distributed on the surface of Fe-NCDs, the responses of the synthesized NCDs to varying pH were investigated (Fig. S8). The PL intensity of Fe-NCDs increases in acidic environments, but is insensitive to alkalinity. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the non-linear relationships between variation (I−I 0)/I 0 of PL intensity of Fe-NCDs versus pH values in the range of 7.0–2.0 are good and clear, and the enhanced extent follows the order of Fe10-NCDs > Fe20-NCDs > Fe30-NCDs. In contrast, the PL of p-NCDs does not significantly respond to varying pH. The different response patterns between Fe-NCDs and p-NCDs are caused by a crucial difference of N speciation on the surface. For Fe10-NCDs as a representative, the high-resolution XPS spectra of N1s of Fe10-NCDs show an apparent decrease of the proportion of unprotonated pyridinic N as the pH value decreases from 7.0 to 3.0 (Fig. 6b) and the C1s spectra reveal an evident rise of CN bond, accompanied with slight changes of other oxygenous groups (Fig. S9). It results in a change of fluorescence decay model and an increase of τ A of Fe10-NCDs (Fig. 6c and Table S5). These are due to the protonation of pyridinic N on the surface of Fe10-NCDs in acidic solution so that the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) process from pyridinic N is suppressed, which contributes to the enhancement of PL. A reversible pH-response of Fe10-NCDs corroborates this dynamic process (Fig. S10). Also, Qian et al. [27] proposed that only pyridinic N among N forms can capture protons and then transfer protons to a conjugated carbon structure which in turn enhances PL of NCDs. As the catalytic effect of Fe3+ becomes more intensive, Fe-NCDs respond to pH changes in a weaker manner, due to the decrease of pyridinic N (Fig. 6a). For p-NCDs (Fig. S11), although the proportion of unprotonated pyridinic N decreases as pH varies from 7.0 to 3.0, the corresponding increase of CN bond cannot be found in C1s spectra and fluorescence lifetime has no significant change. Instead, the ratio of CO to CO increases. These are different from the respond patterns of Fe10-NCDs to protons. It is because the most of pyridinic N of p-NCDs are located inside the core, which make it harder to access protons and improve PL, as a proposal mechanism described in Fig. 6d. It also verifies the ideal model of local structures of Fe-NCDs and p-NCDs as mentioned in Fig. 3d. Such experimental results prove that Fe3+ catalysis gives NCDs a potential for acidity-sensing by controlling speciation and location of nitrogen.

By chelating with imidazole, amino and carboxyl groups of l-histidine, Fe3+ changes the molecular behavior of charing in synthesizing NCDs, which is a common mechanism of metal complex catalysis [58,59]. Actually, Fe3+ does not incorporate into carbon framework, but controls the doping modes of heteroatomic groups. 10% Fe3+ induces a plunge in N/C and O/C ratio of NCDs, even then, the most of N atoms are exposed on the surface, which leads to a sensitivity to protons. As an increase level of Fe3+, percentages of nitrogenous groups ascend, especially pyrrolic N, which results in a prominent enhancement of PL. 30% Fe3+ provides the best effect on optimizing NCDs, which coincides the coordinating stoichiometry of Fe3+ to histidine [60]. Moreover, the effects of other metal ions on synthesis of NCDs were also explored, including Ru3+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Al3+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, and Cd2+ (Fig. 7 ). In addition to Fe3+, Zn2+ and Cd2+ can also prominently enhance PL. Further investigations show that Zn2+ induces changes of UV–Vis absorption characteristic and blue shifts of PL emission as well as an apparent decline of proportion of pyridinic N in XPS and FT-IR results (Fig. S12), which resemble the cases in Fe3+ catalysis. Moreover, several other precursors were also used to derive CDs under Fe3+ catalysis (Table S6). As shown in Fig. S13, Fe3+ induces that more oxygen doping competes with nitrogen in l-lysine-derived NCDs and both N and O speciation undergo apparent alterations, which results in changes of absorption spectra but negligible effects on PL emission spectra. It can be deduced that Fe3+ can control different fractions of heteroatoms in the synthetic processes of CDs, which depends on the composition of starting materials and the types of functional groups. That is, speciation of heteroatoms of CDs can be selectively controlled to tune or improve the properties as long as selecting proper precursors and catalysts.

In summary, it has been proved that Fe3+ catalysis in the synthetic process of NCDs can efficiently control N speciation and enhance QY without any surface passivation. And Fe3+ also induces more aromatic N moieties distributed on the surface of NCDs, which introduces an additional proton-mediated path to further increase PL. Such interesting phenomena confirm that pyrrolic N facilitates PL whereas pyridinic N inhibits it, which is often ignored. Differently from many other reports which regulate heteroatoms doping of CDs by using different precursors, this work employs metal ions to control the structure of NCDs for the first time. Although metal ions are commonly introduced into the synthesis reaction of CDs in many researches [38,61–65], metal/metal ions-functional CDs are always the research outcomes and metal elements are considered to be the cause of diverse properties over there. The mechanism how metal ions affect the structures of CDs which contribute to PL stays unclear. Herein, true and clear mechanism of fluorescence enhancement comes into sight after the mist of metal ion actions blow away. This research should be able to provide an innovative angle on how to manipulate CDs chemical structure as well as insights in a wide range of applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 90922023) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China. We would also like to express our sincere gratitude and appreciation to Yu Ye, for her assistance in spectral measurements, who graduated her Bachelor's degree from Beijing Normal University in 2014.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2016.09.038.
